subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Neutron Stars: Supernova Leftovers
description: A quick, practical guide to Neutron Stars: Supernova Leftovers.

hook.text: When a massive star's core collapses in a supernova, what's left is a neutron star—a city-sized ball of nuclear matter.

buildup.text: A neutron star packs more mass than the Sun into a sphere just 20 km across. A teaspoon weighs a billion tons.

discovery.text: Jocelyn Bell Burnell discovered the first pulsar in 1967—a spinning neutron star emitting regular radio pulses.

twist.text: Some neutron stars spin hundreds of times per second. Millisecond pulsars are the most precise natural clocks in the universe.

climax.text: Magnetars are neutron stars with extreme magnetic fields—a trillion times stronger than Earth's. They can crack their own crust.

punchline.text: A billion tons per teaspoon. Spinning hundreds of times a second.

quiz.question: What is a magnetar?
options: A neutron star with an extremely powerful magnetic field, trillions of times stronger than Earth's
A type of black hole that emits strong magnetic radiation
A star that uses magnetic energy instead of nuclear fusion for power
